description: 'motu' describes someone who assesses their own importance and knowledge without placing themselves above others. Humility permits confidence and expertise; it keeps both open to correction. 'thasu' (modest) concerns how someone presents abilities or achievements, while 'motu' concerns the person's stance towards self and others.
articulatory_notes: Close the lips for voiced 'm', carry the resonance through the nose, and open into stressed true-mid back rounded 'o'. Place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper teeth for dental 't' and release it into high-back rounded 'u'.
sound_symbolism: The closed lips and two rounded vowels keep 'motu' compact. Its unshowy rhythm can help recall a stance that leaves room for other people.
usage_notes: 'motu' can modify a person or stand before a verb such as 'haolu' (speak) to describe manner.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Humility leaves one's account of self open to correction, which supports attention to experience instead of defensive self-display.

semantic_domains.community: a stance that does not place one's importance above other people's
semantic_domains.wisdom: self-appraisal kept open to correction and further learning
